1: The desecration of the bones of the ruling classes — kings, officials, priests, prophets, and people. The exposure of bones (yôṣîʾû ʾet-ʿaṣmôt) was the ultimate post-mortem humiliation in the ancient world; death offered no security from divine judgment. The MKT lists all five categories to show the comprehensive scope: no class of Judahite leadership is exempt.
2: ʾăšer ʾăhēbûm ûʾăšer ʿăbādûm — "which this people loved, served, followed, consulted, and worshipped"; the five-fold description of the sun/moon/stars cult mirrors the five classes of victims in v.1. The MKT preserves all five verbs because together they describe the totality of idolatrous devotion — the same comprehensive energy that should have been directed toward YHWH. "They will not be gathered up or buried" — the non-burial is the ironic punishment for those who worshipped celestial bodies.
3: yibḥar māwet mēḥayyîm — "will prefer death to life"; an inversion of the covenant's life/death choice (Deut 30:19). The survivors of the exile will be in conditions so wretched that death is preferable to survival. The MKT renders the future tense strongly: "will prefer death to life" — the covenant inversion is complete when life becomes intolerable.
4: hăyippōl wĕlōʾ yāqûm — "When people fall, don't they get back up?"; a wisdom observation used as the ground for the rhetorical indictment. The MKT renders the wisdom saying as a question (hă- interrogative), as MT indicates. Natural behavior and common sense confirm the abnormality of Judah's persistent non-repentance.
5: mĕšûbâ nitsaḥat — "perpetual backsliding"; mĕšûbâ is apostasy, turning-away; the nitsaḥat (perpetual, enduring) intensifies it. The MKT renders "turned away in perpetual backsliding" — the repetitiveness of the apostasy is as much the indictment as the apostasy itself. They cling to deception (heḥĕzîqû bĕmirmâ) and refuse to return.
6: ʾên-ʾîš niḥam ʿal-rāʿātô — "no one repents of his wickedness"; nāḥam (repent, relent) is YHWH's own word for emotional turning. Here it is used for Israel's required turning — no one engages in the self-examination that would lead to repentance. The MKT's "No one repents of his wickedness and asks, 'What have I done?'" preserves the introspective question that nobody is asking.
7: ḥăsîdâ... yādĕʿâ mōwʿădêhā — "Even the stork in the sky knows when to migrate"; ḥăsîdâ (stork; its root ḥesed means loyal love — the bird was considered loyal to its young). The migration analogy: birds instinctively follow their appointed times (môʿēd) — but Israel does not follow YHWH's appointed covenant. The MKT preserves the four birds (stork, turtledove, swallow, crane) as a rhetorical accumulation.
8: ʿēt šeqer sōpĕrîm — "the lying pen of the scribes has turned it into a lie"; ʿēt is a scribal pen or stylus; šeqer is falsehood/deception. The scribal class that handles the Torah has itself become the instrument of falsification. The MKT renders "the lying pen of the scribes has turned it into a lie" — the tôrat YHWH that they claim to possess has been adulterated by their scribal handling.
9: ḥāpĕrû ḥăkāmîm — "The wise will be disgraced"; the ḥākāmîm (the wise ones, the sages) are specifically shamed because they claimed the Torah and were trusted with it. The MKT preserves the targeting of the wise class: "they have rejected the word of the LORD — so what wisdom is left in them?" Wisdom that rejects the word of YHWH is self-refuting.
10: The judgment on the prophets and priests who falsify: their property will be redistributed — wives to other men, fields to new owners. The phrase miqqāṭōn wĕʿad-gādôl (from the least to the greatest) applies the judgment comprehensively. The MKT follows MT in specifying both the prophets (nāvîʾ) and the priests (kōhēn) as greedy practitioners of the false-peace message.
11: šālôm šālôm wĕʾên šālôm — "Peace, peace, when there is no peace"; the doubled šālôm is the signature phrase of Jeremiah's critique of false prophecy — also appearing in 6:14. Šālôm denotes comprehensive well-being (health, wholeness, safety, prosperity) not merely absence of conflict. The MKT renders the doubling as "Peace, peace," — the repetition itself performs the falsity of the prophets' assurance.
12: hōbîšû kî tôʿēbâ ʿāśû — "Were they ashamed when they committed these detestable acts?" The answer: they could not even blush (wĕlikhˊlōm lōʾ yādĕʿû). Shame and blush (ḥāpar / kālam) are closely related — the capacity for moral shame has been lost. The MKT preserves both: "no shame... did not even know how to blush" — the double negation emphasizes complete moral insensibility.
13: ʾāsōp ʾăsîpēm — "I will take away their harvest"; an infinitive absolute construction emphasizing the thoroughness of the removal. The agricultural images (no grapes, no figs, withered leaves) reverse the covenant blessings of the land. The MKT follows MT: "no grapes on the vine, no figs on the fig tree, even the leaves will wither" — the complete reversal of land-prosperity.
14: The people's own lament-speech: "Let us flee to the fortified cities" — a despairing acceptance that the judgment announced in v.13 is arriving. The phrase YHWH ʾĕlōhênû hĕdîmānû — "the LORD our God has condemned us" — shows some recognition of YHWH's agency. The MKT renders "condemned us" (rather than "silenced us") following the root dāmam in the sense of bringing to an end.
15: qāwwîtā lĕšālôm wĕʾên ṭôb — "We hoped for peace, but no good has come"; the ironic reversal of the false prophets' promises. The people had been told šālôm would come; instead, there is terror (beʿātâ). The MKT preserves the pair "peace/healing" from the false-prophecy vocabulary and "good/terror" as what actually arrived.
16: miDdan nišmaʿ pinḥat sûsāyw — "From Dan the snorting of their warhorses is heard"; Dan was the northernmost city; Babylon's armies approach from the north. Pinḥat (snorting, loud neighing) creates an onomatopoetic effect. The MKT renders "snorting... stallions' neighing" to capture the two different horse-sounds in the Hebrew.
17: nĕḥāšîm tsipʿōnîm — "poisonous snakes... vipers that cannot be charmed"; a metaphor for the Babylonian army. Ṣipʿōnî is likely the horned viper; the incantation-resistant quality ("cannot be charmed") means no prophetic intervention will deflect this judgment. The MKT preserves "vipers that cannot be charmed" — the snake-charming image underlines that Jeremiah's intercession is futile (cf. 7:16).
18: mabligîtî ʿālê yāgôn — "My sorrow is beyond healing"; the prophet's first-person lament begins. Bālag means to comfort, restore strength; the negated form suggests there is no comfort available. The MKT renders "My sorrow is beyond healing; my heart is faint within me" — identifying these as Jeremiah's own words of empathetic suffering before YHWH's speech resumes.
19: hăʾên YHWH bĕṣiyyôn — "Is the LORD not in Zion?" The people's cry in exile is interrupted by YHWH's counter-question: "Why have they provoked me with their idols?" The MKT preserves this dramatic interruption — the people ask one question, YHWH answers with a different one. "Her King no longer there?" presupposes YHWH as Zion's true king (malkāh).
20: ʿābar qātsîr kālâ qāyits — "The harvest is over, the summer has ended — and we are still not saved"; an agricultural calendar metaphor: the grain harvest (spring) and summer fruit harvest have passed; the year's natural opportunities for survival have closed, yet the salvation the people waited for has not come. The MKT preserves the agricultural imagery because it grounds the lament in the real experience of watching each season pass without relief.
21: ʿal-šeber bat-ʿammî — "I am broken over the brokenness of my people"; wordplay on šābar (break, shatter): the prophet is broken (nišbartî) because of the shattering of the people. The MKT renders "I am broken over the brokenness of my people" — the translation must preserve the echo of the same root to show that the prophet's suffering is a participation in the people's suffering.
22: hăṣōrî ʾên bĕgilʿād — "Is there no balm in Gilead?" Ṣōrî is the resin of the mastic tree, an ancient medicinal substance; Gilead was famous as its source. The rhetorical question assumes the answer is yes, there is balm in Gilead — but the wound of Israel goes unhealed anyway. The MKT preserves the rhetorical question rather than smoothing it into a statement: "Is there no physician there?" — the problem is not lack of remedy but refusal to receive it.
